document.title: Node Browser Canvas Interaction Core (Supersedes v1)
document.purpose: Specify a small reusable Python module that attaches a node-browser style interaction model to a tkinter.Canvas. The module is focused only on canvas interaction, rendering, and a simple projected data model. It is intended to be repointable to different canvas widgets and different data dictionaries.

overview.summary: Implement a procedural, globally-managed canvas interaction system for circular nodes on a tkinter.Canvas. Nodes can be created, dragged, singly selected, marquee-selected in groups, deleted, and connected by curved edges. The system owns only canvas interaction and rendering. External systems may supply the canvas, the data model, and any detail-view behavior.
non-goals: No persistence format in this spec.
No external detail panel behavior in this spec.
No labels or rich metadata beyond node id/name and position.
No zooming, panning, grouping, undo/redo, or multi-canvas synchronization in v1.

architectural-shape: One Python module contains the interaction logic, rendering logic, global state, and active canvas/data references.
The module is externally repointable to a different canvas and/or different data dictionary at runtime.
The module exposes a small procedural surface: attach/use canvas, attach/use data, redraw, and event handlers.
philosophy: Keep the internal data model simple and trusted.
Keep interaction logic direct and easy to inspect.
Structure should be visible from a distance.

module-role.name: Suggested working name: node_canvas_core.py
responsibility: Maintain active references to the current canvas and current graph data.
Handle keyboard and mouse interaction on the active canvas.
Render nodes, selection indicators, marquee rectangle, and connection curves.
Project simple graph data onto the canvas.
external-responsibility: Responding to selection changes if a host program wishes to do so.

data-model.summary: The data model is deliberately small and canvas-oriented.
notes: Node id is both identity and display name for now.
x and y are the logical center of the node.
No other node data is required by this module.
Edges are directional.
Connection rendering is determined entirely from node positions.

global-state.summary: The module keeps a small set of globals representing active canvas, active data, render settings, and interaction state.
rules: Globals are mutated, not rebound wholesale, except when intentionally repointing active canvas or graph data.
Structured state should live in dedicated named globals rather than being stuffed into one catch-all bag.
The module should remain inspectable during runtime.

single-selection.meaning: The node selected for detail view. May be null.

group-selection.meaning: Set of nodes used for group operations such as drag and delete.

notes: Selection state is mutated only through organism-emitted effects.
No behavioral rules are defined here.

event-surface.purpose: Bind toolkit events into RAW input for the interaction system.
notes: Events are translated into RAW state (pointer position, button states, key states).
No interaction behavior is implemented at this layer.
Modes are minimal and advisory.
Organisms may consult mode but do not derive authority from it.
CREATE_NODE is used only by node-create-organism.

node-hit.rule: A point hits a node if its distance from the node center is less than or equal to node_radius plus hit_slop.

priority: Node hit-testing should be based on graph data geometry, not canvas item ids alone.
If multiple nodes overlap, the last-drawn/topmost node should win unless a different policy is later introduced.

interaction-episodes.summary: These describe recognizable interaction patterns over time. They do not grant authority or enforce exclusivity. They are observational and descriptive, not adjudicative.

episodes.name: group-drag
pattern: mouse-down on node in group selection

organisms.summary: Organisms are stateful interaction recognizers that observe input patterns, petition the judge for legitimacy, and emit effects. They do not enforce coordination rules themselves.

pointer-focus-organism.name: pointer-focus-organism
pointer-focus-organism.purpose: Acquire canvas focus on first click without triggering other actions.
effects: consume click (prevent further interaction for this click)
notes: This organism ensures that the first click only establishes focus.
Prevents accidental selection or creation when canvas is not yet active.

node-click-select-organism.name: node-click-select-organism
node-click-select-organism.purpose: Select a single node for detail view.

empty-click-clear-selection-organism.name: empty-click-clear-selection-organism
empty-click-clear-selection-organism.purpose: Clear single-node selection when clicking empty space.

node-drag-organism.name: node-drag-organism
notes: Competes with group-drag-organism; judge determines which is allowed.

group-drag-organism.name: group-drag-organism
group-drag-organism.purpose: Drag all nodes in the current group selection.
recognizes: mouse-down on node in group selection
notes: Takes precedence over node-drag when applicable.

marquee-select-organism.name: marquee-select-organism
marquee-select-organism.purpose: Select nodes within a rectangular region.

edge-create-organism.name: edge-create-organism
edge-create-organism.purpose: Create an edge by SHIFT-dragging from one node to another.
effects: on release over node: create edge
notes: Takes priority over drag organisms when SHIFT is held.

node-create-organism.name: node-create-organism
node-create-organism.purpose: Create a node in CREATE_NODE mode.

delete-organism.name: delete-organism
effects: if group selection exists: delete group
else if single selection exists: delete node

mode-key-organism.name: mode-key-organism
mode-key-organism.purpose: Handle interaction mode changes in response to keyboard input.
notes: This organism centralizes all keyboard-driven mode transitions.
Each recognition branch maps an input pattern to a requested mode.
The organism does not interpret what the mode means; it only sets it.
Additional modes can be added by extending the recognizes list.
Mode changes remain subject to judge approval via interaction-mode resource.

pan-organism.name: pan-organism
pan-organism.purpose: Pan the viewport across an infinite canvas using SHIFT-drag on empty space.
notes: This organism moves the viewport, not the nodes.
It operates only when starting from empty space.
It competes with marquee-select-organism; judge resolves via SHIFT priority.

judge.summary: The judge is the authoritative arbiter of interaction. Organisms may detect and propose actions, but only the judge grants permission and writes coordination state.

resources.pointer: Exclusive ownership of pointer-driven interaction (dragging, marquee, edge creation).
resources.selection:single: Authority to set or clear the single selected node.
resources.group-selection: Authority over the current group selection set.
resources.node:<id>: Exclusive control over a specific node during drag.
resources.edge-create: Exclusive control over edge creation gesture.
interaction-mode: The judge may be implemented as a simple procedural module with explicit checks.
The judge does not need to be generalized beyond the needs of this system.
Coordination state should remain small, inspectable, and easy to reason about.
Organisms remain simple because they do not encode priority or exclusivity rules.

principles: Only one organism may own the pointer at a time.
Resources are exclusive unless explicitly defined otherwise.
The judge is the only writer of coordination state.
Priority rules are applied during permission decisions, not in organism logic.

priority-rules.rule: SHIFT edge creation overrides drag behaviors
priority-rules.effect: edge-create-organism is preferred over node-drag-organism and group-drag-organism when SHIFT is held

priority-rules.rule: Group drag overrides single node drag
priority-rules.effect: group-drag-organism wins over node-drag-organism when node is part of group selection
If pointer is already owned, other organisms cannot START pointer-based interactions
pointer-focus-organism prevents all other organisms from starting on that click

priority-rules.rule: SHIFT + empty drag performs pan instead of marquee
priority-rules.effect: pan-organism is preferred over marquee-select-organism when SHIFT is held on empty space

get_permission.description: Organism requests permission to start or hold resources.

maintain_judge.description: Reconcile coordination state with current organism activity.
responsibilities: Release pointer if no organism is actively holding it
Ensure coordination reflects real active organisms

START.result: allow organism to START if it requests pointer
deny START unless priority rules override
current pointer owner is displaced and new organism takes ownership

HOLD-RESOURCE.condition: resource is held by same organism
resource is held by another organism

notes: The judge may be implemented as a simple procedural module with explicit checks.
The judge does not need to be generalized beyond the needs of this system.
Coordination state should remain small, inspectable, and easy to reason about.
Organisms remain simple because they do not encode priority or exclusivity rules.

effects.summary: Effects are emitted by organisms after judge approval. They are applied to world state or projection.

effect-application.summary: Effect application is the only mechanism that mutates world state or projection.
principles: All state changes occur through applying effects.
No direct domain-specific functions are called by organisms.
Effect handlers are simple and local.

handlers.move-node: Adjust node position by dx, dy.
handlers.pan-viewport: Adjust viewport.offset_x and viewport.offset_y by dx, dy.

rendering-model.summary: Canvas contents are treated as a projection of graph data plus transient interaction overlays.

normal: Outer circle only, white thick outline, no fill or background fill depending on style setting.
selected: Normal node rendering plus dark green interior fill or selected interior effect.
Smaller inner circle at radius 20 with thin outline to indicate selected-node status.
group-selected: Nodes in the group-selected set should display a thin blue halo.
The halo is an additional outer ring slightly larger than the node radius (e.g. radius + 3 pixels).
Halo line width should be thin (1–2 pixels).
Halo color should be a clear blue distinct from selected-node green.
This styling is independent of selected-node styling.

background: Canvas background color should be configurable.
Default may be black for strong contrast with white nodes and edges.
notes: All node and edge positions are transformed by viewport offset before rendering.
World coordinates remain unchanged during panning.

edge-rendering.summary: Connections are directional and visually flow downward from the source node toward the target node.

geometry.start-point: Bottom of source node: (from.x, from.y + node_radius)
geometry.end-point: Top of target node: (to.x, to.y - node_radius)

curve-style.preferred: Smooth cubic-like curve using Canvas smooth line support with manually chosen control points approximated by intermediate points.

notes: The exact curve need not be mathematically pure Bézier if Canvas smooth mode yields the intended visual result.
Arrowheads are optional in v1.
Edges are rendered behind nodes.

coordinate-policy.definition: Node x,y are always center coordinates in canvas space.
movement: The graph data is the source of truth; canvas geometry is regenerated from it.

canvas-binding-model.summary: The module is attachable to a canvas and repointable to another.
behavior: use_canvas(canvas) sets the active canvas reference and binds all required events.
If another canvas was active previously, that old canvas should be unbound first.
use_graph_data(graph_data) sets the active graph reference and redraws.
The module assumes only one active canvas at a time.

optional-callbacks.generate_node_id(): Host may supply new node ids.
optional-callbacks.on_graph_mutated(): Host may be notified after structural or positional changes.

notes: Callbacks are optional. The module should function without them.
Host integration should not be entangled with core interaction logic.

acceptance-criteria: System produces correct effects for node-click-select-organism under valid conditions.
System produces correct movement effects for node-drag-organism and group-drag-organism.
System produces correct group selection from marquee-select-organism.
System produces correct edge creation from edge-create-organism.
Judge correctly enforces pointer exclusivity and priority rules.
World state reflects applied effects accurately.
Projection reflects world state and transient effects.

style: Use procedural functions, named globals, and explicit state.
Prefer geometry-based hit detection from graph_data rather than deriving truth from canvas item ids.
Redraw from graph state rather than incrementally micro-patching every visual detail unless that becomes necessary later.
coding-shape: Small named functions are preferred over one giant event handler.
The code should read like a story of the interaction.

future-extensions: Connection-creation interaction mode, e.g. press C then click source and target.
Distinct visual styling for group-selected nodes.
Node labels drawn next to or inside nodes.

node-creation.summary: Node creation is performed by node-create-organism when interaction context permits.
notes: This section defines intent only.
All behavior is implemented through organisms and judge-mediated effects.

node-id-generation.default: If host does not supply a node id generator, module may generate simple ids like node-0001, node-0002, ...
node-id-generation.preferred: Allow optional external callback or procedure for creating the next node id.

configurability: Auto-select behavior should be implemented in a single clearly named step (e.g. select_single_node(new_id)) so it can be easily removed or toggled later.

focus-and-keyboard.summary: Describes input preconditions relevant to interaction.
notes: Canvas focus is required for keyboard-driven interaction.
Focus acquisition is handled by pointer-focus-organism.
Keyboard input is interpreted only when the canvas has focus.
This section does not define behavior.
